prior.content_md:
## Plain-language summary

MARPOL Annex VI is the binding international treaty controlling air pollution from ships. It was adopted in 1997 and has been substantially revised through successive MEPC resolutions, most recently via the 2021 Revised MARPOL Annex VI (Resolution MEPC.328(76)), which entered into force 1 November 2022.

The annex operates through six principal regulatory tracks:

1. **Sulphur oxide (SOx) limits** — global 0.50% m/m fuel sulphur cap (in force since 1 January 2020); 0.10% m/m within designated Emission Control Areas (ECAs). Ships may alternatively use exhaust gas cleaning systems (scrubbers) achieving equivalent emission reductions.
2. **Nitrogen oxide (NOx) standards** — Tier I, II, and III limits applied to marine diesel engines by build date and operating area. Tier III applies in designated NOx ECAs.
3. **Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)** — minimum energy efficiency standard for new ships by vessel type and size, with phased reduction factors.
4. **Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (EEXI)** — one-time efficiency certification for existing ships; in force from 1 January 2023 certification cycle.
5. **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** — annual operational carbon intensity rating (A through E) for ships above 5,000 GT; in force from 1 January 2023, with corrective action plans triggered by D or E ratings.
6. **IMO Data Collection System (DCS)** — mandatory fuel oil consumption reporting for ships above 5,000 GT, in force since 2019.

*Source: IMO Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships page, IMO.org. [https://www.imo.org/en/ourwork/environment/pages/air-pollution.aspx] — Intergovernmental body position. Source: IMO EEXI and CII FAQ, IMO.org. [https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/eexi-cii-faq.aspx] — Intergovernmental body position.*

## Why it applies to the workspace

The workspace, operating as a freight forwarder, is not a named obligated party under MARPOL Annex VI. The regulation does not impose registration, reporting, or compliance certification requirements on forwarders.

The workspace's exposure arises through three channels:

**Channel 1 — Cost pass-through (ocean-tertiary lanes).** Carrier compliance with EEXI, CII, and sulphur limits imposes operating cost on vessel operators. Those costs — fuel switching to VLSFO or LSFO, scrubber capital and operating expense, slow steaming to improve CII ratings, or speed optimisation — are commercially passed to cargo customers through freight rates and surcharges. The workspace, when arranging ocean freight on Americas–Europe, Americas–Asia, or Europe–Asia lanes for any of its cargo verticals, absorbs or passes on those costs. undefined The magnitude of pass-through is carrier- and lane-specific; current rate is not publicly available in the provided source pool and requires carrier-specific data.

**Channel 2 — Carrier qualification for sustainability-sensitive cargo.** For cargo verticals where clients or end-clients require documented sustainability credentials (live events, luxury goods, film and TV productions with scope 3 commitments, automotive OEM clients), carrier CII ratings and DCS data become relevant to carrier selection decisions. A carrier operating with a D or E CII rating for two consecutive years is required under MARPOL Annex VI to submit a corrective action plan to its flag administration. That signal is publicly visible and relevant to the workspace's carrier vetting process on ocean lanes.

undefined **Channel 3 — Scope 3 emissions reporting exposure.** If the workspace or its clients are subject to emissions disclosure frameworks (CSRD, ISSB, or voluntary GHG Protocol Scope 3), the emissions intensity of ocean legs must be quantified. CII ratings and DCS data are the primary vessel-level data sources for this calculation. For humanitarian cargo verticals where donor reporting includes emissions, and for luxury/automotive clients with public net-zero commitments, this channel is commercially material.

*Source: IMO MARPOL Convention overview, IMO.org. [https://www.imo.org/en/about/conventions/pages/international-convention-for-the-prevention-of-pollution-from-ships-(marpol).aspx] — Intergovernmental body position. Source: IMO EEXI and CII FAQ. [https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/eexi-cii-faq.aspx]*
